examples.title: Access Control List Check Implementation
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating how an ACL-based protection system checks access rights when a process requests access to a file.
examples.code:
PROCEDURE CheckAccess(process, file, requestedAccess)
 // Get ACL for the file
 acl ← GetACL(file)
 
 // Get process's user ID and group ID
 userID ← process.effectiveUID
 groupID ← process.effectiveGID
 
 // Check owner permissions first
 IF file.owner == userID THEN
 allowed ← CheckBits(file.ownerPerm, requestedAccess)
 RETURN allowed
 END IF
 
 // Check group permissions
 IF file.group == groupID THEN
 allowed ← CheckBits(file.groupPerm, requestedAccess)
 RETURN allowed
 END IF
 
 // Check others permissions
 allowed ← CheckBits(file.othersPerm, requestedAccess)
 RETURN allowed
END PROCEDURE

FUNCTION CheckBits(permBits, requested)
 // permBits and requested are masks (R=4, W=2, X=1)
 RETURN (permBits AND requested) == requested
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation: This code shows the hierarchical permission checking in UNIX: first owner, then group, then others. Each check uses bitwise AND to verify if the requested permission is granted.

examples.title: Capability-Based Access Check
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating capability-based protection where each process holds tokens (capabilities) that grant access to objects.
examples.code:
TYPE Capability = RECORD
 objectID: Integer
 rights: Set of {READ, WRITE, EXECUTE}
 // Capability also includes cryptographically secure token
 authenticator: Integer
END RECORD

PROCEDURE AccessCheck(process, object, requestedAccess)
 // Find capability for object in process's capability list
 cap ← FindCapability(process.capabilityList, object.objectID)
 
 IF cap == NULL THEN
 RETURN FALSE // No capability means no access
 END IF
 
 // Verify capability authenticity
 IF NOT VerifyCapability(cap, process) THEN
 RETURN FALSE // Capability may be forged or revoked
 END IF
 
 // Check if requested access is in capability rights
 IF requestedAccess IN cap.rights THEN
 RETURN TRUE
 ELSE
 RETURN FALSE
 END IF
END PROCEDURE

PROCEDURE RevokeAccess(object)
 // To revoke: remove capability from all domains
 // This requires searching all capability lists
 FOR each process IN systemProcessList DO
 RemoveCapability(process.capabilityList, object.objectID)
 END FOR
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: This demonstrates capability-based protection. Access is granted if the process possesses a valid capability containing the requested rights. Revocation is complex as it requires searching all processes' capability lists.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for revocation (n = number of processes), O(1) for access check
examples.spaceComplexity: O(k) where k = number of capabilities per process

examples.title: Domain Switching Implementation
examples.description: Pseudocode showing how protection domains work and how domain switching occurs during system calls in OS.
examples.code:
TYPE Domain = RECORD
 domainID: Integer
 allowedObjects: Map ObjectID → Set of Rights
 isPrivileged: Boolean
END RECORD

TYPE Process = RECORD
 currentDomain: Domain
 // For domain switching
 domainStack: Stack of Domain
END RECORD

PROCEDURE SwitchToDomain(process, newDomain)
 // Check if current domain can switch to newDomain
 IF CanSwitch(process.currentDomain, newDomain) THEN
 // Save current domain
 Push(process.domainStack, process.currentDomain)
 
 // Switch to new domain
 process.currentDomain ← newDomain
 
 // If entering privileged domain, elevate privileges
 IF newDomain.isPrivileged THEN
 EnablePrivilegedMode()
 END IF
 ELSE
 RaiseSecurityException()
 END IF
END PROCEDURE

PROCEDURE ReturnFromDomain(process)
 // Restore previous domain
 IF NOT IsEmpty(process.domainStack) THEN
 oldDomain ← Pop(process.domainStack)
 
 // If leaving privileged domain, drop privileges
 IF process.currentDomain.isPrivileged AND NOT oldDomain.isPrivileged THEN
 DisablePrivilegedMode()
 END IF
 
 process.currentDomain ← oldDomain
 END IF
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: This code shows domain switching - moving from one protection domain to another. Domains are stacked so they can return. When entering a privileged domain, privileges are elevated; leaving drops them (principle of least privilege).
examples.spaceComplexity: O(d) where d = nesting depth of domain switches
